title: Charmed Life - Chapter 12 (Fragment 3)
text:
Janet sighed. “That’s what I mean. I knew you’d get it if I ordered you to. Do you mind not being so kind and obedient? It makes me nervous. Anyway—” She took up the book. “Can you see your face in it?”

“Almost nothing else,” said Cat.

“Funny. I can see my face,” said Janet. “Can I do it, too?”

“You’re more likely to get it to work than I am,” said Cat.

So they both circled the mirror, and they said the words in chorus. The door opened. Mary came in. Janet guiltily put the book behind her back.

“Yes, here he is,” Mary said. She stood aside to let a strange young man come into the room. “This is Will Suggins,” she said. “He’s Euphemia’s young man. He wants to talk to you, Eric.”

Will Suggins was tall and burly and rather handsome. His clothes looked as if he had brushed them carefully after working in a bakery all day. He was not friendly. “It was you turned Euphemia into a frog, was it?” he said to Cat.

“Yes,” said Cat. He dared say nothing else with Mary there.

“You’re rather small,” said Will Suggins. He seemed disappointed about it. “Anyway,” he said, “whatever size you are, I’m not having Euphemia turned into things. I take exception to it. Understand?”

“I’m very sorry,” said Cat. “I won’t do it again.”

“Too right you won’t!” said Will Suggins. “You got off too light over this, by what Mary tells me. I’m going to teach you a lesson you won’t forget in a hurry.”

“No you’re not!” said Janet. She marched up to Will Suggins and pushed Magic for Beginners threateningly towards him. “You’re three times his size, and he’s said he’s sorry. If you touch Cat, I shall—” She took the book out of Will Suggins’ chest in order to leaf hastily through it. “I shall induce complete immobility in the legs and trunk.”

“And very pretty I shall look, I’m sure!” Will Suggins said, much amused. “How are you going to do that without your witchcraft, may I ask? And if you did, I daresay I could get out of it fast enough. I’m a fair warlock myself. Though,” he said, turning to Mary, “you might have warned me he was this small.”

“Not so small where witchcraft and mischief are concerned,” said Mary. “Neither of them are. They’re a pair of real bad lots.”

“Well, I’ll do it by witchcraft then. I’m easy,” said Will Suggins. He searched in the pockets of his slightly floury jacket. “Ah!” he said, and fetched out what seemed to be a lump of dough. For a moment he shaped it vigorously in both powerful hands. Then he rolled it into a ball and threw it at Cat’s feet. It landed on the carpet with a soft plop. Cat looked at it in great apprehension, wondering what it was supposed to do.

“That’ll lie there,” said Will Suggins, “until three o’clock Sunday. Sunday’s a bad time to be at witchcraft, but it’s my free day. I shall be waiting for you then in Bedlam Field, in the form of a tiger. I make a good tiger. You can turn yourself into something as large as you like, or small and fast, if you prefer, and I’ll teach you that lesson whatever you are. But if you don’t come to Bedlam Field in the form of something, that lump of dough will start to work and you’ll be a frog yourself—for as long as I feel like keeping you that way. Right, Mary. I’m through now.”

vocabulary.definition: Anxiety or fear that something bad or unpleasant will happen.
vocabulary.example: She felt a sense of apprehension before the exam.

vocabulary.definition: Large and strong; heavily built.
vocabulary.example: The burly man carried the heavy furniture easily.

vocabulary.definition: A simultaneous utterance of the same words by a group of people.
vocabulary.example: The students repeated the phrase in chorus.

vocabulary.definition: Used to express a confident assumption or guess.
vocabulary.example: I daresay he will agree to help.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling sad or displeased because something hoped for has not happened or been fulfilled.
vocabulary.example: She was disappointed with her exam results.

vocabulary.definition: A case or instance that does not follow a rule or general pattern.
vocabulary.example: Most people agreed with the plan, with a few exceptions.

vocabulary.definition: Covered with or resembling flour in texture or appearance.
vocabulary.example: The baker's hands were floury after kneading dough.

vocabulary.definition: Taking the shape or appearance of a tiger.
vocabulary.example: The spirit appeared in the form of a tiger.

vocabulary.definition: To receive less severe punishment or consequences than expected.
vocabulary.example: He got off too light for such a serious crime.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner that shows or feels guilt or remorse.
vocabulary.example: He looked guiltily at the broken vase.

vocabulary.definition: The state of being unable to move or be moved.
vocabulary.example: After the injury, the patient was in a state of immobility.

vocabulary.definition: To cause a state of total inability to move.
vocabulary.example: The drug can induce complete immobility during surgery.

vocabulary.definition: To cause a state in which movement is impossible.
vocabulary.example: The paralysis induced immobility in the patient.

vocabulary.definition: To cause something to happen or to persuade someone to do something.
vocabulary.example: The medicine can induce sleep quickly.

vocabulary.definition: To look quickly through the pages of a book or magazine.
vocabulary.example: He leafed through the magazine while waiting.

vocabulary.definition: A small mass or chunk of bread or pastry dough.
vocabulary.example: She kneaded the lump of dough before shaping it.

vocabulary.definition: To walk quickly and with determination towards someone or something.
vocabulary.example: He marched up to the stage to deliver his speech.

vocabulary.definition: Playful misbehavior or troublemaking, especially that causes minor harm or annoyance.
vocabulary.example: The children were full of mischief.

vocabulary.definition: Easily agitated or alarmed; anxious or apprehensive about something uncertain.
vocabulary.example: The situation made her nervous.

vocabulary.definition: Willing to comply with orders or requests; submissive to another's authority.
vocabulary.example: She asked him not to be so obedient.

vocabulary.definition: A jacket covered lightly with powder or flour, hinting at a particular profession or activity.
vocabulary.example: The baker's floury jacket showed signs of his profession.

vocabulary.definition: To object strongly to something or feel offended by it.
vocabulary.example: She took exception to his rude remarks.

vocabulary.definition: To punish or reprimand someone to correct their behaviour.
vocabulary.example: He was taught a lesson for cheating on the exam.

vocabulary.definition: Finished; no longer continuing or remaining involved.
vocabulary.example: She said she was through now with that difficult job.

vocabulary.definition: To change oneself into something different, especially by magic or imagination.
vocabulary.example: In the story, the wizard turned himself into a bird.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that involves physical strength, effort, or energy; energetically.
vocabulary.example: She shook her head vigorously.

vocabulary.definition: The practice of magic, especially black magic or sorcery.
vocabulary.example: The villagers accused her of witchcraft.
